name: The Jinn Who Believed
title: The Jinn Who Believed (RA): The Firsts of the Jinn
meta_description: The story of the delegation of jinn who listened to the Prophet Muhammad recite the Quran on his way back from Ta'if, believed, and went to warn their people. A lesson on the unseen fruits of our efforts.
keywords: the jinn who became Muslim, the firsts, jinn in the Quran, Surah al-Ahqaf, Ta'if, night of the jinn, sahaba stories, Dr Omar Suleiman
lede: He was walking home from the hardest day of his life. The people of Ta'if had refused his message and driven him out with stones. He had hoped that a whole town might open its heart, and it had closed against him. As far as he could see, his effort had come to nothing.

This is the story of the jinn who listened to the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ recite the Quran on that road home, believed in it, and hurried back to their people to call them to the truth. They are remembered as the firsts of the jinn (may Allah be pleased with them): a sign sent at the lowest moment, that no sincere effort is ever truly wasted.

paras: The Prophet ﷺ was returning from Ta'if, a journey of about seventy miles on foot, in a time before any easy way to travel it. He had gone there hoping its people would accept the message. Instead he had been mocked, refused, and chased out. He was tired, and he was grieving, and there was no visible sign that the trip had achieved anything at all.

On the way he stopped at a place called Nakhlah, a small spot between Ta'if and Makkah with a little water and some trees for shade. Travellers rested there to drink and to escape the sun. It was the kind of quiet, half-abandoned place where the jinn are said to gather.

It was night, and the Prophet ﷺ stood to pray. He recited the Quran aloud, the way he did in the night prayer. He was not preaching to a crowd. He was simply standing before his Lord, at the end of a long and bitter day, and he did not know that anyone was listening.

Around this time, the heavens had been changing. The jinn who once tried to steal news from the unseen were now being driven back. They understood that something significant had happened to the world, and a search began. Some of them set out to find what it was, the way sincere people search, and others searched only to stop it.

A group of them came upon the Prophet ﷺ as he recited. They stopped, and they listened. One narration describes how they pressed close together to hear, telling one another to be silent. There was no one to introduce them, no crowd to impress. There was only a tired man in the dark, reciting the words of his Lord, and a group of listeners he could not see.
The Quran records the moment Allah revealed it to him. Notice the first word: it was Allah who sent them.

ayah.text: We sent a group of jinn to you [Prophet] to listen to the Quran. When they heard it, they said to one another, 'Be quiet!' Then when it was finished they turned to their community and gave them warning.

sections.h2: What we know about the jinn
paras: The jinn are a creation we cannot see, though they can see us. Among them are believers and disbelievers, just as among people. The Prophet ﷺ taught that some of them are righteous and some are not, and that they too are answerable to Allah for how they respond to His call.

Every prophet sent to humankind was also a warning to the jinn, for they hear our languages and understand the message. The scholars hold that no prophet was ever sent from among the jinn to people; Allah sent messengers from within each community, in its own tongue. And there is no prophet after the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. He is the seal of the prophets, and to believe otherwise is to leave the faith.

What makes this group remarkable is their place in time. They are the firsts of the jinn: the delegation that came to the Prophet ﷺ to embrace his call, before any of the others among their kind. Like the early believers among people, they have their own firsts and their own pioneers, even if their names are known only to Allah. We have nothing to compare them to in our own history, and we do not need it. It is enough to know that the mercy of this message was never meant for one world alone.

sections.h2: They went back to warn their people
paras: The jinn did not keep what they had heard to themselves. They returned to their own people as callers, urging them to believe. They had recognised the truth, and they could not stay silent about it. That is often the first sign that faith has truly entered a heart: it does not sit still.

They did not simply report what they had heard. They urged their people to answer the call, and they warned them of what it would mean to refuse: that whoever turns away from Allah cannot escape Him, and will find no protector besides Him. They carried the message with the seriousness of those who believe it is true.

It is said this group came from Nasibin, which had been a centre of religious learning, a place that still held some reverence for the earlier scriptures. People who already valued the older books were among the first to recognise the new one. The Quran preserves the words they carried home:

ayah.text: They said, 'Our people, we have been listening to a Scripture that came after Moses, confirming previous scriptures, giving guidance to the truth and the straight path.'

paras: Some scholars say there was no immediate conversation that night at Nakhlah. The jinn went back, called their people, and only later did Allah appoint a time for the Prophet ﷺ to speak with them directly. Others gather the narrations differently. The reports must be read together, and on the exact sequence the scholars do not all agree. What is clear is that more than one such meeting is remembered, and that the Prophet ﷺ taught those who came to him.

The best known is the night of the jinn. Ibn Mas'ud (may Allah be pleased with him) used to be asked, "Were you there on the night of the jinn?" He recalled how the Prophet ﷺ had suddenly disappeared, and how the companions searched for him everywhere in alarm. When he returned, he told them a group of jinn had come asking him about Islam, and he had recited the Quran to them and taught them their religion.

There is a valley near Makkah still called the valley of the jinn, close to where this meeting is remembered to have taken place. From this beginning grew a line of believing jinn who would carry the message of the Prophet ﷺ to their own kind, far beyond what any eye could follow.

Ibn Mas'ud later said he once saw, in Iraq, a group of jinn who resembled that same delegation the Prophet ﷺ had taught. The call had travelled. What began with one tired man reciting in the dark had spread among a creation we will never meet.

The surah that records this event carries a related warning to the people of Makkah. They prided themselves on their standing, yet they looked up to the People of the Book and felt themselves below them in knowledge of scripture.

So Allah pointed them to a witness. There would come a man from the Children of Israel, a learned rabbi of Medina, who would recognise this message and believe in it, while the proud of Makkah turned away. The people you least expect may accept the truth, and the people who seem most certain may refuse it.

It is a pattern that runs through the whole story. The townsfolk of Ta'if refused. A delegation of unseen jinn believed. A scholar of another faith, in another city, would believe. Guidance does not follow our expectations. It follows the will of Allah, who reaches whom He wills.

Step back and see what Allah was doing. After Ta'if, the Prophet ﷺ felt rejected and humiliated by the people he had come to help. Then, in the same season of his life, he was taken up through the heavens on the night journey, welcomed and greeted by the prophets before him. After being turned away by the elites of one town, he was honoured by the noblest of all creation. And on the ground, Allah sent him a delegation of jinn who believed when the people of Ta'if would not. The greater the hardship, the greater the mercy placed beside it.

The lesson is gentle and it is heavy. You may pour yourself into something good and see nothing come of it. The people in front of you may turn away. And all the while, Allah may be reaching hearts you will never meet, planting a seed whose fruit appears long after you are gone.

The same surah, named after the sand dunes where the prophet Hud once warned his people, reminds Makkah of earlier prophets who were refused and whose people were destroyed. Yet the lessons of those prophets survived to guide others. Effort that looks wasted in the moment can become guidance for someone else, in a time you cannot see.

It is the experience of countless people: someone accepts faith years later because of a Muslim they once met, a kindness or an honest word whose effect was completely hidden at the time. The two never meet again. The one who planted the seed may never know. This is how Allah works, and it is why no sincere effort is ever truly lost.

lessons_intro: This is not a story about strange creatures. It is a story about the quiet ways Allah answers a sincere heart, even when the answer is hidden from view.

lessons.text: The Prophet ﷺ left Ta'if seeming to have failed, and on that very road Allah sent him people who believed. You rarely see the whole result of what you do.

lessons.bold: Seeds bear fruit on Allah's schedule, not ours.
lessons.text: A kind word, a patient example, an honest stand: these can reach a heart years later, long after you have forgotten the moment.

lessons.bold: When one door closes, look for the mercy in it.
lessons.text: Rejection by people came paired with honour in the heavens and belief among the unseen. Hardship rarely arrives without ease beside it.
The jinn did not keep their faith private. They hurried back to warn their people. What we truly believe, we want to share.

faq.q: Who were the jinn who became Muslim?
faq.a: They were a delegation of jinn who came upon the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ as he recited the Quran in the night, on his way back from Ta'if. They believed in the message and returned to their own people to call them to it. They are remembered as the firsts of the jinn.

faq.q: Where in the Quran is this mentioned?
faq.a: The event is described in Surah al-Ahqaf (chapter 46), where Allah tells the Prophet ﷺ that He sent a group of jinn to listen to the Quran, and that they returned to warn their people. There is also a chapter named Surah al-Jinn (chapter 72).

faq.q: Are there believers among the jinn?
faq.a: Yes. The Prophet ﷺ taught that among the jinn are believers and disbelievers, just as among people. They are a creation we cannot see, they are accountable to Allah, and every prophet sent to humankind was also a warning to them.

faq.q: What can we learn from this story?
faq.a: That no sincere effort is ever truly wasted. The Prophet ﷺ left Ta'if seeming to have failed, yet Allah was reaching hearts he could not see. We plant seeds whose fruit may appear long after we are gone.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: Most of us know the feeling of trying hard at something good and watching it seem to come to nothing. We speak, and no one listens. We give, and nothing changes. It is easy to decide the effort was wasted and to stop.

This story is a quiet correction. On the worst day of the Prophet's life, when the people he came to help had turned him away, Allah was at work where he could not see, drawing in hearts he would never have expected. The harvest was never ours to control, only the planting. Do the good thing, say the true word, set the patient example, and leave the rest to Allah. You are rarely shown the whole of what your effort becomes, and the part you cannot see may be the largest part of all. Carry that trust into today, and this story has done its work.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of the jinn who believed in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). Quran translations are from The Qur'an, M.A.S. Abdel Haleem (Quran 46:29 and 46:30). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

slides:
He was walking home from the hardest day of his life.

Ta'if had refused him and driven him out with stones.

As far as he could see, his effort had come to nothing.

He stopped to rest at a quiet place called Nakhlah.

It was night. He stood to pray, and recited the Quran aloud.

He did not know anyone was listening.

A group of jinn came upon him as he recited.

They stopped. They pressed close to hear, telling one another to be silent.

"We sent a group of jinn to you to listen to the Quran." (Quran 46:29)

They believed.

And they did not keep it to themselves. They hurried back to their own people to warn them and call them to the truth.

"Our people, we have been listening to a Scripture that came after Moses, confirming previous scriptures, guiding to the truth." (Quran 46:30)

These are remembered as the firsts of the jinn.

See what Allah was doing.

The people of Ta'if turned him away.

The same night, Allah sent him hearts that believed: hearts he could not even see.

**Your effort is never wasted.**

You may pour yourself into something good and see nothing.

And all the while, Allah may be reaching someone you will never meet.

Do the good thing. Say the true word.

Leave the harvest to Allah.

May Allah be pleased with them.
social.caption:
On the way home from Ta'if, after the hardest rejection of his life, the Prophet ﷺ stopped to pray in the night and recited the Quran aloud. He did not know that a group of jinn had stopped to listen.

They believed. Then they hurried back to their own people to call them to the truth. They are remembered as the firsts of the jinn (may Allah be pleased with them).

The lesson is gentle and heavy at once: on the day his effort looked wasted, Allah was reaching hearts he could not see. You are rarely shown the whole of what your good becomes.

Which part of this stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who needs it today.

email.subject: The day his effort looked wasted
email.alt_subject: Someone was listening that he could not see
email.preview: On the road home from Ta'if, a group of jinn stopped to hear the Quran, and believed.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

The Prophet ﷺ was walking home from the hardest day of his life. He had gone to Ta'if hoping its people would accept his message, and instead they refused him and drove him out. The journey back was about seventy miles on foot. As far as he could see, his effort had come to nothing.

He stopped to rest at a quiet place called Nakhlah, between Ta'if and Makkah. It was night, so he stood to pray, and he recited the Quran aloud, the way he did in the night prayer. He was not preaching to a crowd. He was simply standing before his Lord at the end of a bitter day, and he did not know that anyone was listening.

A group of jinn came upon him as he recited. They are a creation we cannot see, though they can see us. They stopped, and they pressed close to hear, telling one another to be silent. Allah later revealed the moment in the Quran: "We sent a group of jinn to you to listen to the Quran. When they heard it, they said to one another, 'Be quiet!'"

They believed. And they did not keep it to themselves. They hurried back to their own people to warn them and call them to the truth. They are remembered as the firsts of the jinn.

Step back and see what Allah was doing. After Ta'if, the Prophet ﷺ felt rejected and humiliated. He could see only the people who had turned him away. And in that same night, Allah sent him hearts that believed, hearts he could not even see.

There is a lesson here that is gentle and heavy at once. You may pour yourself into something good and watch nothing come of it. The people in front of you may turn away. And all the while, Allah may be reaching someone you will never meet, planting a seed whose fruit appears long after you are gone.

So do the good thing. Say the true word. Set the patient example. Then leave the harvest to Allah. You are rarely shown the whole of what your effort becomes, and with Allah nothing sincere is ever lost.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: The Prophet ﷺ was walking home from the hardest day of his life. He had gone to Ta'if hoping its people would accept his message, and instead they had mocked him, refused him, and chased him out with stones. As far as he could see, his effort had come to nothing.

On the way he stopped to rest at a quiet place called Nakhlah, a small spot with a little water and some trees for shade. It was night, so he stood to pray, and he recited the Quran aloud. He did not know that anyone was listening.

A group of jinn came upon him as he recited. They are a creation we cannot see, though they can see us, and among them are believers just as among people. They stopped, and they pressed close to hear, telling one another to be silent until he had finished.

They believed in what they heard. And they did not keep it to themselves. They hurried back to their own people, calling them to the truth: "Our people, we have been listening to a Scripture that came after Moses, confirming previous scriptures, guiding to the truth." They are remembered as the firsts of the jinn.

Later, Allah appointed a time for the Prophet ﷺ to meet some of them and teach them. Ibn Mas'ud recalled the night the Prophet ﷺ suddenly disappeared, how the companions searched for him in alarm, and how he returned to say that a group of jinn had come asking him about Islam.

Step back and see what Allah was doing. On the day the people of Ta'if turned him away, Allah was reaching hearts he could not even see. The Prophet ﷺ felt his effort had failed, and in the same night a delegation of the unseen came to believe.
storybook.pullquote: We sent a group of jinn to you to listen to the Quran.
storybook.reflection: We all know the feeling of trying hard at something good and seeing nothing come of it. This story is a quiet correction. On the worst day of his life, when the people in front of him had turned away, Allah was at work where the Prophet ﷺ could not see. Do the good thing and leave the harvest to Allah. You are rarely shown the whole of what your effort becomes.
storybook.source: This story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
